reasoning: The 30-day average over the past 360 days is ~5.333, which maps to ~64 fatalities annually. ACLED data for Greece shows fatalities are driven almost entirely by highly clustered migrant shipwreck events (e.g., 16 in April 2025, 8 in Nov 2024, 4 in Sept 2024). Because this distribution is heavily right-skewed, the median monthly casualty count is near 0. To exceed the mean (need >=6), a specific mass-casualty event must occur, which historically happens only ~2-4 times a year (roughly a 20-30% base rate for any given 30-day window). For the Aug 10 resolution, 75% of the window is already in the past (mid-July to early-Aug), and web searches reveal no major shipwreck events, so the probability of getting >=6 deaths in the remaining few days is very low. For future windows (Sep, Nov, Jan), the base rate of ~25% perfectly applies, but probabilities are pulled progressively closer to 0.5 to account for longer-term uncertainty (e.g., changing migration enforcement policies or extreme weather).
